title: Ziziphora infecta
taxonomy: Ziziphora infecta is a species of flowering plant in the family Lamiaceae, commonly known as mint family. It belongs to the genus Ziziphora, which consists of several species of annual or biennial plants. The family Lamiaceae is a large and diverse group of plants, including many well-known herbs and ornamental species. Ziziphora infecta is native to the Middle East and Central Asia. Its classification is further specified at the rank of species, with the full botanical name being Ziziphora infecta.
uses: Ziziphora infecta, also known as Persian balm or mountain mint, has been used in traditional medicine for its antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties. The plant's essential oil is used to treat various health issues, including digestive problems and respiratory infections. It is also believed to have antimicrobial properties, making it effective against certain types of bacteria and fungi. In addition, Ziziphora infecta has been used to treat skin conditions, such as acne and wounds, due to its antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties. Further research is needed to fully understand the medicinal uses of Ziziphora infecta.

There is no information available on the plant Ziziphora infecta. However, I can provide information on Ziziphora taurica, which is a plant species from the same genus.

Ziziphora taurica, also known as Persian balm, is a perennial herbaceous plant that belongs to the mint family (Lamiaceae). It has been traditionally used for its health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and expectorant properties, which can help relieve respiratory issues such as coughs and colds. The bioactive constituents of Ziziphora taurica include essential oils, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, Ziziphora taurica is often used to make teas, infusions, and decoctions, as well as being incorporated into various herbal preparations such as syrups and cough drops.
